content.problemStatement:
NNN points are given on the coordinate system such that the coordinates of the ithi^{th}ith point are (Xi,Yi)(X_i,Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) and Xi<Xi+1X_i \lt X_{i+1}Xi​<Xi+1​ for all (1≤i<N)(1\le i \lt N)(1≤i<N).

Maintain two types of queries:


Type 111: You are given 555 integers in the form: `1 L R K B`. For all L≤i≤RL \le i \le RL≤i≤R, change the coordinates of the ithi^{th}ith point to (Xi,K⋅Xi+B)(X_i, K\cdot X_i+B)(Xi​,K⋅Xi​+B).
Type 222: You are given 333 integers in the form: `2 L R`. Find whether the Lth,(L+1)th,…,RthL^{th}, (L+1)^{th},\ldots ,R^{th}Lth,(L+1)th,…,Rth points are collinear (lie on the same straight line).

Input Format

The first line of input contains two integers NNN and MMM, which represent the number of points and the number of operations.
The next NNN lines, each line contains two space-separated integers: Xi,YiX_i,Y_iXi​,Yi​, the coordinates of the ithi^{th}ith point.
The next MMM lines, each line contains a query in the above discussed format.


Output Format
For each query of type 222, if points are collinear, print `YES`. Otherwise, print `NO`.


The output is case-insensitive. Thus, the strings `YES`, `yes`, `yeS`, and `Yes` are all considered the same.
constraints: During the process Yi may exceed 10^9, but will always fit into 64‑bit signed integer.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers N and M – the number of points and the number of queries. The next N lines each contain two integers Xi Yi – the coordinates of the i‑th point. It is guaranteed that X1 < X2 < … < XN. The following M lines describe the queries:
- Type 1: "1 L R K B" – for every i with L ≤ i ≤ R set Yi = K·Xi + B (Xi never changes).
- Type 2: "2 L R" – answer whether the points with indices L … R are collinear.
All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each query of type 2 output a line containing "YES" if the selected points are collinear, otherwise output "NO". The case of the letters does not matter.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Query 111: A single point is considered collinear.
Query 222: Coordinates (0,0)(0,0)(0,0) and (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2) lie on the same line, i.e. y=xy = xy=x.
Query 333: The three points do not lie on the same line.
Query 444: We change the third point from (3,4)(3, 4)(3,4) to (3,1⋅3+0)=(3,3)(3, 1\cdot 3 + 0) = (3, 3)(3,1⋅3+0)=(3,3). Similarly fourth point is changed from (5,4)(5, 4)(5,4) to (5,5⋅1+0)=(5,5)(5, 5\cdot 1 + 0) = (5, 5)(5,5⋅1+0)=(5,5).
Query 555: The first five points lie on the same straight line, i.e. y=xy = xy=x.
Query 666: We change the fourth point from (5,5)(5, 5)(5,5) to (5,5⋅(−1)+12)=(5,7)(5, 5\cdot (-1) + 12) = (5, 7)(5,5⋅(−1)+12)=(5,7).
Query 777: The last three points lie on the same straight line, i.e. y=−x+12y = -x+12y=−x+12.

solution.approach:
Observations:
1. Points are collinear iff all consecutive slopes are equal. For i in [L, R‑1] define slope Si = (Y_{i+1}-Y_i)/(X_{i+1}-X_i). The answer to a type‑2 query is YES iff all Si for i∈[L,R‑1] are identical (a single point is always collinear).
2. An update of type‑1 overwrites Yi on a whole interval with the linear function f(x)=K·x+B. Consequently:
   • For every internal edge i∈[L,R‑1] the new slope becomes K (because (K·X_{i+1}+B)-(K·X_i+B)=K·(X_{i+1}-X_i)).
   • Only the two border edges (L‑1) and R may change to a value that depends on the neighbour that is *outside* the updated interval.
3. Therefore we need two data structures:
   • A segment tree Tval that stores Yi and supports range assignment of a linear function (K,B). It allows point queries to obtain the current Yi of any index.
   • A segment tree Tslope that stores for each edge i (1 ≤ i < N) the slope Si as a reduced fraction (num, den). The tree supports:
        – range assignment of a constant slope (K/1) on an interval of edges,
        – point update of a single edge when one of its endpoints changes,
        – query on a range of edges returning whether the whole segment is uniform and, if so, which slope it is.
   The node of Tslope keeps:
        uniform (bool) – true if the whole segment has the same slope,
        num, den – the normalized numerator and denominator of that slope (den > 0).
   Merging two children: the segment is uniform only when both children are uniform and their slopes are equal.
4. Processing a query:
   • Type 1 (L,R,K,B):
        – Apply range assignment (K,B) on Tval for indices [L,R].
        – In Tslope assign slope K/1 on edges [L, R‑1].
        – Re‑compute edge L‑1 (if L>1) using Yi_{L‑1} from Tval and Yi_L = K·X_L+B, then update that single edge in Tslope.
        – Re‑compute edge R (if R<N) using Yi_R = K·X_R+B and Yi_{R+1} from Tval, then update that edge.
   • Type 2 (L,R):
        – If L==R answer YES.
        – Query Tslope on edges [L, R‑1]; if the returned node is uniform answer YES, otherwise NO.
5. All operations are O(log N). The whole algorithm works in O((N+M)·log N) time and O(N) memory.

Normalization of a slope: given Δy and Δx (>0) we compute g = gcd(|Δy|,Δx) and store (Δy/g, Δx/g). Equality of two slopes is then simple integer equality.

The implementation below follows the described structures and respects the requirement of looping over a dummy test‑case counter (T=1).
